So I happen to get myself here and kind of want to say something…

My basic stance is no, a script section is not needed. You see, tools like NPM has a script section not because this is a core feature, but they *need* it—there is no viable alternative anywhere in JavaScript, Ruby, etc. Python, on the other hand, has like 100 command runners available on PyPI (I’m probably vastly underestimating), including very mature, popular, and resourceful ones like Fabric and Invoke. There’s no need to go into this well-trotted field and add more fragmentation. You can just, say, add `invoke` to your `dev-packages`, and run your jobs with `pipenv run inv start`.

One thing I think that might be interesting though, is to do allow custom subcommands like Git does, e.g. `git mycoolcommand` automatically delegates to command `git-mycoolcommand` if there is one. This way people can start extending Pipenv with their own ideas, and if some prove to be extremely useful and popular Pipenv can always turn them into core subcommands.

> pyproject.toml isn't really supported for library definition metadata -- and not at all by any version of pip that doesn't implement peps 517 and 518 (both of which are still having implementation details worked out) as an authoritative library declaration file. setup.cfg exists for that purpose (the actual successor to setup.py ) and IMHO both of those should really be supported.

Well this is certainly off topic but it's an important discussion so I can't help myself.

There is actually no standard around `setup.cfg` right now other than the conventions established by distutils and setuptools. `pyproject.toml` is absolutely for library metadata as the successor to `setup.py` or the community would have placed build requirements in `setup.cfg` instead. 

`pyproject.toml` describes how to build a project (PEP 518), and part of building is describing metadata. I'm NOT saying that `pyproject.toml` needs a standard location for this metadata, but PEP 518 uses this file to install a build tool and from there it's very reasonable to expect that the build tool will use declarative configuration from somewhere else in the file to determine how to build the project.

Anyway, going back to pipenv vs poetry - there seems to be some idea floating around that applications don't need certain features that libraries get, like entry points, and this is just incorrect. It should be straightforward for an application to be a python package.

The only true difference between an application and a library in my experience with python and with other ecosystems is whether you're using a lockfile or not. Of course there's a third case where you really just want a `requirements.txt` or `Pipfile` and no actual code and that seems to be all that pipenv has focused on so far (`pipenv install -e .` falls into this category as pipenv is still afraid to try and support the package metadata). Unfortunately, while the design of pipenv is cleaner with this approach, it's also way less useful for most applications because PEP 518 decided to punt on how to install projects into editable mode so in order to continue using pipenv we will be stuck on setuptools quite a while longer as you cannot use `pyproject.toml` to switch away from setuptools and still use `pipenv install -e .`.

@techalchemy Calculating the dependency tree for the to-be-upgraded component and then merging it with the existing lock file is the approach that makes sense to me.

So if I'm understanding the status quo correctly, I think that would leave me with two questions:

- when calculating the fresh dependency tree for a selectively upgraded package, does the end result match `pip`'s `only-if-needed` upgrade strategy, or does it match the `eager-upgrade` strategy? While the latter would still be better than eagerly upgrading everything, the former is more what I had in mind when I proposed the `--selective-upgrade` idea (I've changed jobs since then though, and am now in a situation where I'm more likely to do batch updates, which is why I haven't really been tracking the feature's evolution over time)
- regardless of exactly how selective upgrade works, does it make sense to expose a way to request the same "override and/or unpin these versions" logic as part of the `pipenv lock` API? At the moment, `--keep-outdated` isn't flexible enough to let `pipenv install --selective-upgrade ...` be expressed as an equivalent `pipenv lock --keep-outdated --some-other-override-args... && pipenv sync` operation

@ncoghlan I agree with you that it is almost certainly related to unlinking open FDs, my current efforts around this include reimplementing the explicit attempt to finalize weakrefs that was included in the `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` implementation as well as explicitly terminating process groups recursively using psutil.

The pip codebase itself acknowledges that repository indexes are cloned in read-only mode, but our calls to `pip install` are done via `delegator` which uses `popen` with `shell=True`. On windows that is known to leak file descriptors as it spawns an entirely new process group.

I have a reimplementation of all of these things but instead of ensuring an empty directory *during* the process, I am getting the tests passing by ensuring that the directories are _completely removed_ at the end of the process.  Here is a proof of concept, I'll have a working patch shortly:

```python
    def test_editable_vcs_install(self):
        my_temp_dir = None
        with PipenvInstance() as p:
            with temp_environ():
                with TemporaryDirectory(prefix='pipenv', suffix='tmp') as tmp_dir:
                    os.environ['TMPDIR'] = '{}'.format(tmp_dir)
                    my_temp_dir = '{}'.format(tmp_dir)
                    other_temp_dir = '{}'.format(p.path)
                    c = p.pipenv('install -e git+https://github.com/requests/requests.git#egg=requests')
                    assert c.return_code == 0
                    assert 'requests' in p.pipfile['packages']
                    assert 'git' in p.pipfile['packages']['requests']
                    assert 'editable' in p.pipfile['packages']['requests']
                    assert 'editable' in p.lockfile['default']['requests']
                    assert 'chardet' in p.lockfile['default']
                    assert 'idna' in p.lockfile['default']
                    assert 'urllib3' in p.lockfile['default']
                    assert 'certifi' in p.lockfile['default']
        assert not os.path.exists(my_temp_dir)

@giulianob The example in the beginning of this issue breaks up these bytestrings into the parts denoted in the [protocol documentation](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol#AGuideToTheKafkaProtocol-MetadataAPI). The newline you see in the `bytearray` example isn't actually a newline, it's just some byte sequence representing the length of the string `dummytopic` that happens to also be the ascii code for `\n` (the number 10 represented as a bytestring).

Instead of writing a utility, this change can actually be as simple as taking a test that looks like this
```python
bytearray(b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x01\x00\ndummytopic\x00\x00\x00\x0ctestuserdata')
```
and reformatting it as this
```python
bytearray(b'\x00\x00'  # version
                b'\x00\x00\x00\x01'  # len(topic names)
                b'\x00\n'  # len(topic name)
                b'dummytopic'  # topic name
                b'\x00\x00\x00\x0c'  # len(user data)
                b'testuserdata')  # user data
```
by using this specification as a reference:
```
ProtocolMetadata => Version Subscription UserData
  Version => int16
  Subscription => [Topic]
    Topic => string
  UserData => bytes
```

I can assure you errorhandler doesn't do anything of the sort, check the code, there's not much to it:

https://github.com/Simplistix/errorhandler/blob/master/errorhandler/__init__.py

So, having had a look at greenelab/manubot#2, you're hitting the confusing behaviour of the Python logging library as documented here:

https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#logging.basicConfig

Previously, you were doing no setup, so your first call to `logging.(error|warning|etc)` called `basicConfig`, but that does nothing as once `ErrorHandler()` is installed, the root logger already has a handler.

Moral of the story: always explicitly configure your log handlers ;-)

Interestingly Subutai, this is a use case for the functional reactive
library I was describing before.

If you have a caller that must react to every call by handling some output,
(even when you have moving windows which don't output anything useful for
some period) - you must handle this in your client code, making the client
code really knowledgable of the context of every call to the target methods.

However, if we use a FRP Library such as RxJava (there is a Python and C++
version too), the chain of clients taking subsequent output don't receive
anything until something useful is available, then they do their processing
without any knowledge of how many items to "skip" or any such "God"
perspective observation.

Not only this, but all "objects" along the method call chain are atomic and
asynchronously protected.

Imagine this. You have a chain of functions like:  x,y -->  (x + y), (x /
y), ([the 4th x later] + y), (x + [y if it ever becomes available])
where each pair of parenthesis is another function.

Now say you have a thread which is processing the user's mouse movements
across the screen.

The usual way to deal with this is to have semaphores and thread blocking
(to make sure you get a legitimate value out of x and y).

With FRP you can just get the value of x and y whenever and don't care how
much the user's mouse is moving or anything - plus there's no thread
contention, deadlocks or race conditions - whatever x and y happen to be at
the time is what get applied with each function and each function doesn't
care about the previous or next.

Imagine combining Network nodes with this semantic? We could have all sorts
of asynchronicity (if that's a word) - and not care...

I think this could be interesting, especially for streaming clients...  ;-)

David

I followed up on the offline discussion that I had with @scottpurdy concerning breaking out of the propagationDelay setting from `linkParams` into a separate arg. My investigation found that there would be numerous additional touch-points if we break it out. This includes, but is not limited to, changes in:

1. NetworkFactory (deserialization from YAML)
2. Link serialization (both flavors - old and new)
3. Link deserialization  (both flavors - old and new?)
4. Network.link() arg changes
5. Link::Link constructor arg changes
6. Bindings
7. Python Network.Link API changes
8. Corresponding unit test work

The existing 'linkParams' YAML string arg is presently used to pass only linkPolicy params.

So, pondering the tradeoffs between:
1. Using the existing 'linkParams' YAML string arg to pass a combination of link and linkPolicy params. This gets us most of the above for free; i.e., no changes besides new tests for the new feature.
2. Break out the `propagationDelay` arg into a separate arg in various places. This incurs changes in the touch-points enumerated above. Is it worth it?

The initial reason for building the checks for the backends was, that for older keyring implementations (pre 8.0), it would by default select an unencrypted backend and simply store your plaintext password on disk. This would happen on linux/ubuntu if the ``gi`` module was not available. This module contains the python bindings for the PyGobject introspection which can not be installed via ``pip`` but only via ``apt``. So, for example, this would happen if you made a virtualenv without using ``--site-packages`` with ``afp-cli`` and installed ``keyring``. Hence I built this hacky check to make sure that naive users wouldn't store their password unencrypted on disk.

Newer versions of keyring (>8.0) no longer have such behaviour and instead raise the runtime exception mentioned above if they couldn't find a desirable backend. The desirable backends are now:

```
        keyring.backends.kwallet.DBusKeyring
        keyring.backends.OS_X.Keyring
        keyring.backends.SecretService.Keyring
        keyring.backends.Windows.WinVaultKeyring
```
as stated on: https://github.com/jaraco/keyring/blob/master/CHANGES.rst

Added all this, comes the fact, that the keyring module is still considerd an ``experimental`` feature of the ``afp-cli`` ans is in fact not yet listed in the dependencies of the ``build.py`` and as such will not be installed or upgraded automatically...

In REST API we can choice if it should return:
 1. list of keys only
 2. list of values only
 3. list of key/values

This is an example of knowledge `Faq`:

![screenshot - 03272015 - 10 18 30 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/377047/6865290/95bb0370-d46c-11e4-8f5b-d7566aa6be2d.png)


1. Returns keys:

```bash
$> http GET http://0.0.0.0:8080/api/knowledge/faq/mappings/from
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Length: 26
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Fri, 27 Mar 2015 09:23:43 GMT
Server: Werkzeug/0.10.1 Python/2.7.6

[
    "banana", 
    "foo", 
    "apple"
]
```

2. Returns values: in this case return a empty string. 
If necessary, we can add an argument to ignore empty values.

```bash
$> http GET http://0.0.0.0:8080/api/knowledge/faq/mappings/to
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Length: 20
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Fri, 27 Mar 2015 09:24:43 GMT
Server: Werkzeug/0.10.1 Python/2.7.6

[
    "", 
    "bar", 
    "fruit"
]
```

3. Returns key/value pairs: if value is not defined, it returns an empty string.

```bash
$> http GET http://0.0.0.0:8080/api/knowledge/faq
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Length: 94
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Fri, 27 Mar 2015 09:23:07 GMT
Server: Werkzeug/0.10.1 Python/2.7.6

[
    {
        "from": "banana", 
        "to": ""
    }, 
    {
        "from": "foo", 
        "to": "bar"
    }, 
    {
        "from": "apple", 
        "to": "fruit"
    }
]
```

You can use [`incr_dbuilder`](https://patsy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/API-reference.html#patsy.incr_dbuilder) or [`incr_dbuilders`](https://patsy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/API-reference.html#patsy.incr_dbuilders) (depending on whether you want to give it `y` values as well -- it's the same distinction as `dmatrix` versus `dmatrices`). They're design to work with data that's too large to fit into memory, so instead of giving them your data directly, you give them a function that returns an iterable over "chunks" of your data. If your data fits into memory (just the design matrix doesn't), then you could do something simple like:
```
def iter_maker():
    return [my_data_frame]
design_info = incr_dbuilder("a + b + log(x)", iter_maker)
```
and now `design_info` is a [`DesignInfo`](https://patsy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/API-reference.html#patsy.DesignInfo) object with a `column_names` attribute.

If your raw data is too large to fit into memory, you can change `iter_maker` so that it returns an iterable over chunks of your data where each chunk is small enough to fit into memory. Writing a generator is often the easiest way to do this -- something like:
```
def iter_maker():
    CHUNK_SIZE = 1000
    offset = 0
    while offset < total_rows:
        yield fetch_rows_from_database(offset=offset, size=CHUNK_SIZE)
```

I'm not sure what use the column names are without the design matrix... I would be curious to hear what you're going to do with them :-)

Notice that once you have a `DesignInfo`, you can use this to generate individual "slices" of your design matrix, so you can pass it to algorithms that can process design matrix incrementally.
```
chunk_of_design_matrix = dmatrix(design_info, chunk_of_data)
```

(NB: this answer assumes you're using patsy 0.4 or greater; everything is still possible with earlier versions but 0.4.0 simplified things by combining what used to be two different classes into a single `DesignInfo` class, so some details may be different.)

floats in all cases. They will have bee converted from Angles to radians upstream.  (cf. `_posToImage` in wcs.py, which is the function that will call `_xy`)

A few other places, we've introduced variants that have a leading underscore to skip the normal overhead.  e.g. `_Image(array, bounds, wcs)` creates an image with those values with very little overhead. The situation today for the `cotsoc` part, is that a preprocessing file rewrites the whole param hierarchy, and makes understanding and contributing a mess. But it is necessary at the moment, for the params have multiple categories expressed through tree levels (*cotisation patronale* / *cotisation salarié*, *cotisations privé* / *cotisation fonctionnaire*, *cadre / non-cadre*).

Some of these categories are OpenFisca input variables (*cadre/non-cadre/public*, *taille entreprise* which duplicates baremes) and could be filtered dynamically if correclty expressed in the param file. 
Others correspond to *sum variables* (e.g. `class cotisations_employeur`), or non-existing variables that may still have an interest for categorizing params (e.g. `cotisations URSSAF`) that may be implemented with tags, letting us flatten the param file.

Several tests with http://www.cnn.com on Memento-compliant archives **actually do** return a *memento* relation, indicating that it is available for acquiring the Memento-Datetime. So, in those case, the Memento-Datetime is accessible from the TimeGate response. If the Accept-Datetime is not submitted as a header, then no *memento* relation is present, but there is *last memento* which can be used. Obviously, TimeGates for noncompliant archives, such as http://webcitation.org, will not have this relation, so there is no way to get this information. I looked at RFC-7089 and it **does not** require a *memento* (or *last memento*) relation in the Link header for Pattern 1.1 or 2.1, so sites may not consistently list this relation, requiring one to make a request of the URI-M for the Memento-Datetime. The only required relation is *original*, and it explicitly states that *The response MUST NOT contain a "Memento-Datetime" header*. The method get_memento_info holds close to this spec. The static parse_link_header function takes the Link header and breaks it into a Python dictionary. The method __prepare_memento_response allows you to pass in the link header as a string along with the URI-M, the Memento-Datetime, and a status code for the URI-M. So, with all of this in mind, do you see any issues with this procedure? 1. perform the request on the URI-G with the Accept-Datetime 2. get the URI-M from the Location header in the response 3. parse the link header using parse_link_header to get the Memento-Datetime from the *memento* relation or the *memento last* relation, depending on whether Accept-Datetime was submitted 4. use __prepare_memento_response, passing it the URI-M, the Memento-Datetime, and the Link header to produce the JSON response This gets almost everything needed to fill in the JSON. The only item lacking is the *http_status_code*, which you will not know until the URI-M is dereferenced. It is set to *None* if not passed to __prepare_memento_response. Take care of the error handling and I think this will work. :-D

Hello,

Try using the header: `Contenty-Type: application/json`.

When there is defined a content type, Flask using Request.form[1] 
to provide the POST parameters, and sets when it uses the Request.data[2] 
for this purpose.

The means "safe" pick up via Request.form parameters is:

```
list(request.form.to_dict().keys())[0]
```

and the text is encoded and failure to capture the parameters.

For example:

```python
@jsonrpc.method('App.hello')
def hello(name):
    return u'Hello {0}'.format(name)
```

```
$ curl -i -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
>   -d '{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "App.hello", "params": ["Flask=JSONRPC"], "id": "1"}' \
>   http://localhost:5000/api
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 72
Server: Werkzeug/0.10.4 Python/2.7.10
Date: Wed, 29 Jul 2015 14:07:25 GMT

{
  "id": "1", 
  "jsonrpc": "2.0", 
  "result": "Hello Flask=JSONRPC"
}
```

```
$ curl -i -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
>   -d '{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "App.hello", "params": ["Flask+JSONRPC"], "id": "1"}' \
>   http://localhost:5000/api
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 72
Server: Werkzeug/0.10.4 Python/2.7.10
Date: Wed, 29 Jul 2015 14:07:43 GMT

{
  "id": "1", 
  "jsonrpc": "2.0", 
  "result": "Hello Flask+JSONRPC"
}
```

Thank you.

[2] - http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.10/api/#flask.Request.data

To me, the interesting question is how it would integrate with unit testing framework. At the moment, `testtools.TestCase` has a `useFixture` method which using the current, synchronous `Fixture` interface.

Within a test, `f = useFixture(SomeFixture())` is more or less equivalent to:

```python
f = SomeFixture()
f.setUp()
self.addCleanup(f.cleanUp)
```

If you had asynchronous code, it'd look like this:

```python
f = AsyncFixture()  # this doesn't actually exist for real anywhere
d = f.setUp()
d.addBoth(lambda _: self.addCleanup(f.cleanUp))  # maybe addCallback?
...
```

But, that assumes your `addCleanup` is already geared to handle whatever asynchrony `f.cleanUp` returns.

For Twisted support, rather than subclassing, testtools has a really nice compositional abstraction for controlling how tests are run called `RunTests`. e.g. 

```python
class TwistedTests(TestCase):
    run_tests_with = AsynchronousDeferredRunTest()

    def test_foo(self):
        d = deferLater(5)
        d.addCallback(lambda _: 20)
        return d.addCallback(self.assertEqual, 20)
```

Currently, the `RunTest` object is unavailable to test code, and doesn't modify any environments that test code can access (modulo shenanigans). We could change that, maybe. I mean, it's arguably a bug that `TestCase.addCleanup` schedules synchronously on tests that use `ADRT`. Still, the right interface isn't clear to me. Play & input are required.

For other test frameworks that dwell in the benighted lands of reuse-by-inheritance, they would need some other way saying "this test uses this (deferred|effect|asyncio|vanilla) fixture".

```python
from pkg_resources impor parse_version
import fontTools

assert parse_version(fontTools.version) >= parse_version("3.19")

Any command line flag that spark-submit has today (or that will be added in the future) is there because, presumably, someone had a use case. Since this is OSS that is meant for the general public, I don't think we should assume that no one would ever want to pass `--properties-file` or `--proxy-user`. This also applies to `PYSPARK_SUBMIT_ARGS` itself which we are blocking.

Few more thoughts:
1. Appending to PYSPARK_SUBMIT_ARGS is very unlikely to cause non-theoretical breakage (I am willing to bet no users have this var set).
2. I think we should not replicate existing Spark features unnecessarily. This just adds more walls for the users to run into, instead of being able to rely on Spark and its documentation.  Specifically, overriding `PYSPARK_SUBMIT_ARGS` is a surprising behavior (I'd consider this a bug)
3. Certain Spark configuration parameters are comma-separated lists so the proposed implementation will not handle them correctly without adding escaping rules.

Another possible implementation (though I like it less) is to introduce `SPARKLY_SUBMIT_ARGS` that appends to `PYSPARK_SUBMIT_ARGS` - this should address the concern of potential breakage, but would also passing through arbitrary flags, and won't suffer from the commas issue.

@dhermes Thank you for the prompt consideration!  Good points.  I did not consider the pubsub.Topic() and pubsub.Subscription() factories since they are not used in the documentation examples. We can fix that bit.  

That said, this still seems really not super great: 
1.  There is an asymetry here: pubsub.Client().topic() but a subscription but a subscription as pubsub.Client().topic().subscription()?  
2.  You could argue that pubsub.Subscription() and pubsub.Topic() are the right way to create these, but both of these factories take a client as an argument. So you still have to do: 
`
 client = pubsub.Client()
 subscription = pubsub.Subscription(sub_name, client=client) 
`
Which would have been at least consistent, but we provide a much more comfortable interface for the topic: topic = pubsub.Client().topic() which leads me to expect the same for a subscription and also ask why would there be two separate interfaces for creating a subscription (or a topic) one of which is mildly (the pubsub.Topic(name, client=pubsub.Client()) and the other is inconsistent between Topic and Subscriber. The way this was solved in Java is to have separate data and admin classes (Subscriber and SubscriberAdmin).  Perhaps a better thing to do is to say that Client() holds all admin operations, like list_topic() and list_subscription() as well as factories for creating subscribers and topics.


>  Won't the topic be embedded in the subscription resource name? 
True. This is not an issue of keeping the topic name from the user. It's about not requiring the user to know the topic name in order to create a subscription in the first place.

Today, when a user logs in with OS Login, the PAM module will create a file in a particular directory to grant the user sudo permission (assuming the user is authorized). If we get a server error authorizing a user, check if the user has a file associated with them. Deny the user login if they do.

@yugangw-msft Besides their [subtle conceptual difference](https://docs.python.org/2/howto/logging.html#when-to-use-logging):

> warnings.warn() in library code if the issue is avoidable and the client application should be modified to eliminate the warning

> logging.warning() if there is nothing the client application can do about the situation, but the event should still be noted

One noticeable difference is `for i in range(10): warnings.warn("foo")` will only print out one output by default. And my guess is, this exact smart feature causes the test counter not counting properly (in Python 2.7.9?), hence the test failure.

Using `logging` instead, or setting `warnings` to "always warning" mode, would pass that test case, but it results in tons of [duplicated warning messages](https://travis-ci.org/AzureAD/azure-activedirectory-library-for-python/jobs/176898120) showing up on the console when running our unit tests.

In this particular case, I guess I would simply relax that assertion to make the test cases pass. After all, it is just some optional warning message, for a behavior which will eventually be replaced by next major version update.

```
import sys

from pants.util.process_handler import subprocess

if __name__ == '__main__':
  with open('output.txt', 'w') as f:
    sys.stdout = f
    print('Doing a thing in the parent.')
    subprocess.check_call(['echo', 'Echo to default destination.'])
    subprocess.check_call(['echo', 'Echo to explicit destination.'], stdout=sys.stdout)
```

The AdaptiveLearningRateSchedulers available in Sockeye use a method called `new_evaluation_result(has_improved: bool)` which controls the learning rate based on whether the optimized metric did improve on the validation set at the current checkpoint. 
With distributed training, you have multiple training processes running independently on multiple hosts. If you use a standard Sockeye training processes as workers, each of these processes run their own validation iterator on each checkpoint and may set `has_improved` differently.
Moreover, to my knowledge, there is no way to set the learning rate of the global optimizer that is pickled and sent to the MXNet distributed training server. 
But I am not too familiar with this part of MXNet, so maybe this has changed by now.

Anyway, for multi-host Sockeye training runs, one would need to define a master process that runs validation at each checkpoint and controls the global learning rate based on that. All other workers should skip this.

When you assign to Run.text, it deletes all the contents of that run (including any images) and replaces it with the text you assign.

You could append text to the run using Run.add_text(). Otherwise you would have to go down to the lxml level underneath the Run and operate on individual `<w:t>` (text) elements.

The `w:r` run element is available on Run._r, and it has a .t_lst element that will get you the w:t elements under it, then I believe those have a read/write .text property you can use.

@shin- I don't think this is an issue with requests, it _looks_ like docker-py assumes chunks will be at the JSON object boundary, and that something at the Docker API level has broken that previously-true assumption. Obviously there is no such guarantee from Requests, Docker (according to the docs), or HTTP. It looks like Docker is using "almost" [line-delimited JSON](http://jsonlines.org/), but with the difference that it sends CRLFs instead of just LFs.

I think the best solution to this would be to continually receive chunks into a buffer, and then attempt to consume CRLF delimited JSON objects out of that buffer. docker-py should be able to handle receiving a chunk that contains multiple JSON objects, or a chunk that doesn't contain any valid JSON and must be concatenated with past or future blocks for the JSON to be valid.

Thanks for your patience, I was on vacation and could not reply earlier. Distributing ODBC drivers via conda sounds like a great idea. This would also solve issues around different versions of unixodbc/iodbc on the host system and the conda environment.

ODBC drivers are usually distributed via one of the following methods:
* Packages that come with the operating system. Examples: MySQL and PostgreSQL drivers on most Linux systems, MSSQL ODBC driver on Windows
* Installers/packages that contain ODBC drivers for installation with/without adminstrator rights. Examples: MSSQL ODBC drivers on Linux
* Source packages that contain code for compilation with your system's ODBC headers. Examples: Many open source DBs, including MySQL, PostgreSQL, ...

Maintaining conda packages may be a legal hassle depending on the driver's license. The best solution would be to convince as many package maintainers/companies as possible that providing Conda packages for their driver is a good idea.

I could try to convince the Exasol company to supply their Exasolution ODBC drivers as Conda packages, and I would be willing to invest limited time in that. For other drivers, I am afraid I will not be able to justify the time investment to my employer, and I will not be sufficiently motivated to do it in my spare time (seeing I do not use these databases or can use the existing packages).

@mcg1969 your [comment above](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/4956#issuecomment-291720963) explaining why hotfixes are necessary is very helpful.  I appreciate the comments following on from that by @jasongrout and @minrk.  I understand there are further conda-team-internal discussions that are happening as well.

### Summary

Below I introduce terms for 5 principles: Containment, Invariance, Equivalence, Deprecation, Precedence.  Using these I propose that packages once published are never changed allowing MD5 sums to stay the same for all time, but that a published package can, optionally, be superceded by a new package with a higher build number and meta-data specifying that it supercedes and deprecates the previous package.  Deprecated packages are labelled as such in the repository and therefore will effectively cease to be available.  Mirroring mechanisms should pick up these changes.  Local package caches and conda environments containing the deprecated package will warn the user when they realize they contain a deprecated version that has been superceded by a functionally equivalent package.

### Details

Here is my view:

1. I really don't think we can change MD5s for a publicly released package -- implying for a fixed channel, package name, platform, version, build number.  Replication and caching of packages sits at the core of the Internet and even the way conda works.  We break all sorts of implicit (and well founded) assumptions when we start doing this.  The purpose of generating these MD5 sums is to verify that the package has not been changed: either by corruption, mirroring fault, or maliciously.  MD5 sums are a standard way for people to verify that they have mirrored and retrieved the "correct" version (which is why we use them and why conda aborts when it discovers an MD5 sum mis-match).  The possibility of a package changing, once published, undermines the ability of millions of people to trust conda.

2. The meta-data has an intimate relationship with the behavior of the package.  The meta-data provides an operational specification that needs to be satisfied.  The degree to which this is "pinned" can be decided, to a degree, independently, but whatever decision is made, and whoever makes that decision, the end user of the package, in most cases, wants to be sure they have received an exact copy of that decision.  The most practical way to do this is to i) embed the meta-data into the conda package; and ii) use a single MD5 sum as the combined "package + meta-data" verification mechanism.  A whole host of other complications will be introduced if the meta-data is separated, verified, and linked/synchronized independently from the code-only (or binary-only) package.  I feel like those arguments (granted, described here in an entirely un-rigorous manner) may undermine the "build group" proposal which otherwise has some things to recommend it.

3. The issues outlined above by @mcg1969 are totally legitimate: packages that were 100% correct at time of creation and valid when used to create an environment should not be made invalid (i.e. guaranteed to not work) by the release of a dependency package that makes it no longer compatible with the base package, despite the base packages now-out-of-date dependency specification suggesting that it should be valid.  Environments containing those packages should not be exposed to the risk that updates will result in the environment being broken.

This is a tricky set of conditions to satisfy, but let me try and provide some terminology for principles that can be used to build a proposal:

1. Containment: meta-data is part of the package and needs to be embedded inside the package as the canonical version describing the content and specification.  The reason is that the meta-data provides information that impacts the way the package behaves.

2. Invariance: a specific release of a package, once issued, must never change.  Correspondingly its MD5 sum will never change (given a standard and normalized packaging mechanism, such as tar-bz2 -- and as a side note, I'm not actually sure conda does normalize package contents before the tar-bz2 step).  The reason is that conda specifically and the Internet in general rely on package caching and a (not unreasonable) assumption that things like versioned software packages won't change once released publicly, coupled with the use of MD5 sums to verify this invariance.

3. Deprecation: a mechanism to make it clear that a previously issued package is deprecated and should not be used.

4. Equivalence: a standard for specifying functional package equivalence.  I believe packages that only vary by build numbers (in meta-data) satisfy this.

5. Precedence: a standard for understanding package release (partial) ordering.  I believe we already have this (otherwise I don't know how conda's solver would work).

@mcg1969 has outlined a number of scenarios above that are not edge cases but very accurately represent present reality.  They come down to "The package base content is just fine, but the package meta-data is now invalid due to some new conditions which have arisen, and as such can result in environment creation or package updates/installs that will be invalid".  Here is a proposal for what I think should happens when this event occurs:

1. A new package with an incremented build number is created.  We need to create a new package anyway, the only difference from the current hotfix process is that this is new, not a replacement.  The new package can explicitly list the old packages that it deprecates, perhaps in a way that conda can leverage (in the creation of a new repodata.json or when running the solver).  This will satisfy the principles of invariance (old package isn't overwritten), equivalence (new package is functionally the same as old package), and precedence (new package is used in preference to old package).  

2. The old package is removed from standard package channels and moved to a "deprecated" channel.  In this way the package is still accessible and can be mirrored if required, but it will not be picked up by the solver.  Today I do not know what the implication is for end-user package caches.  This (partially) solves the principle of deprecation (removing the old package from the standard package resolution set).

3. Some TBD mechanism is used to flag the package as deprecated.  This flag would allow package caches to flag the deprecated packages as "dirty" and treat them differently when doing local package updates and installations.  This would be new conda/repo functionality.  This solves the rest of the principle of deprecation.

Implications:

1. Anyone doing new/fresh package installs or environment creation will only pick up the "corrected" package

2. Anyone wishing to apply this strategy will need to manage two channels: the "main" channel and the "deprecated" channel.  Somewhat as an implementation detail Anaconda Repository (as it stands today) makes this very easy: labels can be used to create (effectively) new (sub) channels for things like "alpha", "beta", "rc1", "dev", and, if required, "deprecated" within any "top level" channel.

3. Anyone with the old package installed in an environment that previously worked would need to have conda realize that the old package is deprecated and that a new package needs to be installed in its place.  If it is a read-only environment then conda would need to flag this (deprecation warning) and either abort (unless a flag such as `--allow-deprecated` is used) or proceed (unless a flag such as `--deny-deprecated` is used), depending what is decided for the default behavior.   Based on PLS I would suggest that the default should be to abort on the basis that the package was deprecated for a reason and the user would need to `--allow-deprecated` to continue, understanding this may produce undesirable results.

4. It may make sense to have an option to `update-deprecated`, such that any time package operations of any sort are applied to a conda environment that has packages in it which are known to be deprecated those packages will be updated to their superceded functional equivalent package, thereby bringing into effect the updated meta-data and dependency specifications.

5. It reduces the number of packages that are considered "live" or "valid" at any given time.

6. conda would be updated to have a formal understanding of build number as a higher precedence equivalent (if it doesn't already have this understanding).  Some configuration mechanism would allow users to (optionally) specify a requirement to ALWAYS use a higher build number if it is available.

7. In this world it should be the exception, not the norm, that a package's dependency specification  would ever contain a build number.  Instead the specs should assume the principles of "equivalence" and "precedence" and make their package dependency specifications open to *any* build for the given version.

8. None of this implies that a new build number **necessarily** causes an older build to be deprecated, which I think was one of @minrk 's concerns about the "build-group" proposal.

Concerns:

1. Lots of packages will be shuffled into this "deprecated" state.

2. How far back in previous package versions will this process be applied?  Has the risk of being extensive.

@jjhelmus and I have discussed #7454 and #7548 separately.  Jonathan tried to remove the code in the conda solver that minimizes the number of features.  That then restores "normal" behavior.  This is clearly not a workable solution, though, because we depend on that optimization to prioritize the MKL package such that it is installed by default whenever no specific blas metapackage is specified.  Still, it seems highly unintuitive that this optimization should trump channel priority.

@mcg1969 can you please comment on thoughts behind this feature minimization taking precedence over channel priority?  Is this intentional?  Do we have use cases where we depend on this behavior?

> the new activate scripts

The usage of awk and dirname are not new:

https://github.com/conda/conda-env/blame/develop/bin/activate#L32

That's conda-env, of course, but that's where the scripts that are now in conda came from.

Something else is going wrong.  It might be a bug in the path translation stuff.  It is supposed to just be a pass-through on *nix to *nix, but I did see some issues like this in fixing the tests.  I'll look into it and see if I can improve the tests to catch whatever is happening here.
OK.  I don't appreciate your snark, but I respect your opinion, and hope that this decision works out OK for our users.

I think this is a duplicate of #1961 - my suggestion remains the same: run your long-running processes with a separate Miniconda installation.  You will never be able to update files on Windows when they are in use.

Using ``eval`` works - however this is not the real solution here - something happened in conda v4.1.4 that broke the (otherwise valid) way to do it that we are using.

We do use the pinned file for other dependencies, and I agree that in the future we could use it for python and numpy too.

The reason why these options don't solve the issue is that we are setting up ``$CONDA_INSTALL`` inside the ``ci-helpers`` package, and many packages are now potentially using the ``$CONDA_INSTALL`` variable inside their own Travis files, so we need to get this working as it did before, otherwise we face the prospect of updating quite a few .travis.yml files.

For now, we are pinning conda to 4.1.3.

I was able to reproduce the problem---it is specifically limited to installing tarballs _in the package cache_. As a short-term fix you can copy the package out of the cache and then reinstall, I think. #2907 is the same issue. I'm working on a fix now. cc: @kalefranz

@bashtage I thought it was replaced by @mingwandroid:
> The lastest minicondas have been temporarily replaced with the previous one while we name a fix here.

> It might also be interesting to save file hashes when untarring a package, and to have a command that tests the package cache or an environment for modified files.

We're moving in that direction.  `conda build` now includes an `info/paths.json` that has sha256 and byte size for each file in the package.  That file consolidates and supersedes `info/files`, `info/has_prefix`, and a few others.

The next step is to get that information written to the conda-meta directory, along with the new shasum if the path was updated (i.e. prefixes rewritten) when linked into the environment.

I'd also like to suggest that work on **build string** syntax and semantics shoud be a much higher priority than work on version comparison. This would vastly improve conda's ability to reason about *binary* compatibilty of *compiled* packages. Observe that version numbers are an attribute of the *source code*. While version numbers have implications on binary compatibility (especially when semantic versioning is used), neither concept completely covers the other. Conda already recognizes this by encoding important binary properties of a package into the build string, e.g. `py27_vc11_1`, but this mechanism appears to be entirely ad-hoc. This woudn't be a problem if build strings were merely informative, but they do in fact take part in version comparison as a ternary comparison criterion (see `conda/resolve.py` lines 388 and 396). Since build string order is just lexicographic order, and the internal structure of build strings is poorly standardized, the outcome of these comparisons is very fragile. 

Even build number comparison (the secondary criterion) is affected by poor build string standardization, because build number parsing fails in certain situations: If the code has not changed in the meantime, the build number 2 is correctly extracted from a build string consisting of a number and a git hash like `2_3af2853`, but if the hash happens to contain only digits like `2_12345`, the build number becomes 212345. Conda works around this behavior by prefixing hashes with the letter `g`, but this is again ad-hoc and poorly documented.

I propose to define syntax and semantics of build strings with comparable expressivity (but different meaning) as version numbers, and to implement build string comparison with the same sophistication as version comparison. This would empower recipe designers to express constraints on binary package properties as precisely as is already possible with version numbers for source properties.

> I intended to only install Miktex and dependencies that I didn't have yet. I didn't expect conda to be updated from the conda-forge channel.

Rather than the command

    conda install -c conda-forge Miktex

the correct command for what you want is

    conda install conda-forge::miktex

That will do exactly what you're asking.  The first command applies `conda-forge` as a higher channel priority over `defaults` for the entire environment.

Using *just* `sudo` alone, without fully switching users, will often get you into trouble with conda.  We're working to clean that up, but it's not a trivial problem.  Right now, best practice is to *fully* switch users, not just invoke sudo to get another user's permissions.  So, something like

@bioinformed  I finally had some time to look into this properly. I think we can get relative paths to work in every situation (egg/wheel/local install), but I made a slight change to the setup: instead of using `@loader_path` directly in the install name, I reverted to using `@rpath` for the install name prefix, and setting `rpath` on the library to `@loader_path`. For packages that run inside Python, this is functionally identical to what I had before, but gives some flexibility to 3rd party applications that want to link against pysam: they can provide `-rpath` upon compilation to specify the pysam install location. Is that acceptable?

This dance with `@rpath` and `@loader_path` is similar to how Conda and Enthought EPD packages are built; see https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/279. The fix that I have here is also very similar to the existing [bioconda recipe for pysam](https://github.com/bioconda/bioconda-recipes/blob/master/recipes/pysam/osx_rpath.patch), though that doesn't include the provision with `@rpath`.

To test this, I ran `python setup.py develop`, `python setup.py install`, and I installed from a platform wheel, each time in a clean environment. I then ran the tests, and I installed htseq and ran the tests for that package. In each case I got a clean test run (with one caveat, described below). I was also able to dynamically load the pysam libraries from an external C program, though I had to set `-rpath` when compiling.

The only issue that I had while testing was that there has been a new version of samtools/htslib/... in the last week or so. If this new version is installed in the environment, it interferes with the version of htslib which is vendorized with pysam. To work around this, I pinned the version numbers of samtools etc. to 1.4 in the travis setup script (2bf0869). I haven't properly investigated what is going on here.

One perhaps overly specific use case would be if you wanted to issue an elasticsearch query within a pyspark map.  Much of the time using something like elasticsearch-hadoop and then doing the processing in pyspark is more performant, but occasionally I've found cases where the queries for each row are sufficiently different and decoupled to where it makes more sense to have query per row in a map. 

If you do that, you have to open and close a lot of connections quickly (where normally maybe you wouldn't do that kind of thing), so waiting for the connection to get closed by gc is less than ideal.  We end up with much more reliable jobs in that case with the explicit closure shown above. 

As for adding it to transport vs. client, I could see adding it to both, but as a user, I'd rather interact with just the client when possible rather than have to munge around lower level objects, especially for something as high-level as this.  Any particular reason why not to include it at the client level (possibly in addition to transport)?

Also out of curiosity, why no context managers?

There is a `bulk` helper that greatly simplifies the format needed - http://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.io/en/master/helpers.html#bulk-helpers

Today value sources is only used by vm/vmss create:

```
--image                       : OS image (URN alias, URN or URI).  Default: Win2012R2Datacenter.
        URN aliases: CentOS, CoreOS, Debian, openSUSE, RHEL, SLES, UbuntuLTS, Win2008SP1,
        Win2012Datacenter, Win2012R2Datacenter.
        Example URN: MicrosoftWindowsServer:WindowsServer:2012-R2-Datacenter:latest.
        Example URI: http://<storageAccount>.blob.core.windows.net/vhds/osdiskimage.vhd.
        Values from: az vm image list, az vm image show.
```

If there was no long description is would look like this:
```
--image                       : OS image (URN alias, URN or URI).  Default: Win2012R2Datacenter.
        Values from: az vm image list, az vm image show.
```
In this case, value sources shouldn't appear in the long description field.  They should be in the short description field so we don't unnecessarily break the help flow

Hi @kemura, fail_on_exist has the default value of False for the following reason:

When you call the Storage REST API to create a container which already exists, an error would be returned. This is inconvenient as in most use cases, the user does not care whether the container existed when they tried creating it, they just want to make sure the container exists before creating blobs under it, so the SDK swallows this error [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/master/azure-storage-blob/azure/storage/blob/baseblobservice.py#L601) when fail_on_exist is set/defaulted to False.

This is the desired default behavior, but the user can change it by setting fail_on_exist to True if they want to know whether the container existed when they tried creating it. 

Please let me know if you have any other question.

from azure.storage.queue.queueservice import QueueService

```
from azure.storage.queue import QueueService

Hmm taking a look at a few things still.

The public ssh key is not actually part of pool create process. The `userAccounts` property on the pool is only for this "user impersonation" feature -  so the public key can't be inserted into this particular json file. You can however supply it in a json file to the user create command. Where your json will look like this:
```
{
    "name":"myuseraccount1",
    "isAdmin":true,  
    "password":"mypassword",  
    "expiryTime": "2017-08-01T23:00:00.000Z",
    "sshPublicKey": "mysshkey"
} 
```

This can then be added with an additional command:
```
az batch pool create --json-file my_pool.json
az batch node user create --pool-id <pool_id> --node-id <node_id> --json-file my_user_settings.json
```

Hey guys - as @randymeech mentioned I have created a library https://github.com/openvenues/address_normalizer which handles most or all of these cases without losing information. 

At the moment we produce all the combinations (Dr expands to either Drive or Doctor, potentially to Dominican Republic as well depending on which gazetteers are being used) and give you back a few variants on the string without trying to resolve the ambiguity. This is suitable for search indexing and deduping, but not necessarily for displaying addresses and formatting. One option is to keep the original text as the display address but index the normalized form(s) in search/dbs. That's currently what we do in deduping (first surface form into the index wins). I've also been thinking about a statistical NLP approach which would label one candidate expansion as more likely than the others using a sequence model, just need to construct a small training set of ambiguous strings and their correct normalized forms.

Our library also does a lot more than just synonym expansion. It includes:
- a lexer-based tokenizer which looks for multiple patterns simultaneously and classifies each token type as it goes through the text (that runs at C speed). Plays nicely with unicode
- normalization of diacritical marks (é => e, etc.) which can be turned off as you prefer
- Multi-word numeric expression and ordinal normalization ("One twenty-first" => 121st)
- An efficient trie data structure which allows us to look up millions of distinct multi-word phrases e.g. from GeoNames in a single pass through the text while using on the order of 100M of memory or so. This allows us to reason about address components found in text/webpages and helps with picking apart combined fields in certain data sets.
- A (soon-to-be-implemented) slight variant on this trie allows us to expand German street suffixes which can either be written as a separate word or tacked onto the street name (Nord Mauerstraße => Nord Mauer straße or Nord Mauer strasse).

There's a new version that hasn't been pushed to Github yet which is written in (modern) C, default bindings in Python and easy to bind to other languages like Node, etc. The included gazetteers are just text files and can be easily edited collaboratively where it makes sense or you can BYO expansions or mix and match. I've also written some code to download the latest GeoNames and add all the alternate=>canonical names to the trie which helps with expanding city aliases e.g. NYC => New York City, ATL => Atlanta. They also have a lot of cross-lingual toponyms e.g. Milan/Milano which is very useful from an l10n/i18n perspective.

The currently deployed (mostly Python) code is running in production and is deduping millions of addresses across several data sources including OpenAddresses and OSM.

Feel free to review, use, fork or contribute to our repo (I'm renaming it libpostal for the new release). I'm hoping it can be shared by multiple organizations and combine best practices in address normalization, extraction and parsing. Let me know if that sounds useful/interesting to you guys.

While I think it would be really nice to do grading in the live notebook, I'm still not convinced it is something that can really replace formgrader right now.

Currently, the formgrader stores the results in the database (through the use of a REST api) rather than in the notebook itself. This is really a critical feature for several reasons:

* The database is critical because it allows instructors to easily get out grades in, for example, a CSV format. To do this takes only a few seconds under the current implementation, if you had to go through all the notebooks and extract the metadata from them this could easily take 10 minutes if you have a large class.
* If you keep the database but also want to store metadata in the notebooks, then (as you mentioned) this introduces an additional step of having to run something that goes through all the notebooks, after formgrading, and pulls out the grades and saves them in the database. Instructors then have to remember to run this step every time they make any changes when grading.
* Not having information saved directly in the database (i.e., while grading) makes it really difficult to determine what notebooks have or have not been graded. A very commonly used feature of the formgrader is to go through and grade all of one part of a notebook, then the next part of the same notebook, and so on. This means that you have a bunch of notebooks that are all in a partial state of grading and the formgrader makes it easy to identify where you need pick up where you left off. This would be difficult to present if all of the information is only stored in the metadata, again because you'd have to iterate through all the notebooks and pull out the metadata every time you want to show that information.
* The formgrader presents a lot of really useful information that will pretty much be impossible to display without a database being updated in realtime. For example, you can see what the average grade on each problem is, how many students failed the test cases, whether any assignments have been "flagged" (e.g. for cheating or other reasons), etc. These could still be displayed in some UI drawing from the database, but again if you have to run a separate command to update the database, then this information will frequently be out-of-date. I forsee a lot of instructors forgetting to run the command to refresh the database.

Those critiques aside, I'm happy to explore the idea of doing live grading, and maybe we could even offer a "lightweight" version of the grading interface that works in the live notebook. But, I don't think we can retire the formgrader entirely.

If I interpret [this line](https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_client/blob/master/jupyter_client/launcher.py#L69) in `launcher.py` (jupyter_client) correctly, the kernel will inherit the notebook server's environment, unless the kernel spec defines an environment. So, if your kernel specs don't set any environment variables, you could provide what you need to the notebook server, and it will be available to the kernels.

If your kernel specs do set some environment variables, you could customize the launcher to pass selected environment variables from the notebook server to the kernels. Or you could customize the kernel manager to always pass an environment definition to the launcher. If you get the list of environment variables to be propagated from the configuration, you could create a PR and maybe get your changes merged.

To summarize, it's a variant of [this idea](https://github.com/schematics/schematics/issues/324#issuecomment-155924477) that already got tentative support, but instead of using `to_primitive` and `to_native` as the universal interface I landed on `export` and `convert`.

The reason is that the *format* (primitive/native) is just a detail that is subordinate to the *process* (import/export). Compound types don't care about the format at all. It's also more flexible this way, 'cause now the formats aren't hardcoded in the interface. A custom type can implement a third format if it wants.

This is what `ListType.export` (formerly `export_loop`) looks like now:

```python
def export(self, list_instance, format, context):
    data = []
    for value in list_instance:
        # Just call `export` on the field; no more inspecting for `export_loop`.
        shaped = self.field.export(value, format, context)
        # Filter out empty values as before...
        ...elif shaped is not None:
            data.append(shaped)
    return data
```

When the process reaches the atomic field, `BaseType.export()` will call either `self.to_primitive()` or `self.to_native()` based on the format.

There are "only" two breaking changes:

* Custom compound types must implement the new interface by turning `to_native` into `convert` and `export_loop` into `export`, but this is very straightforward.

* `Model.to_native()` now actually returns a native value (instance) instead of a dict.

Packing and unpacking container types (like lists) can get tricky because we can't intercept all the Python syntax for making lists (like literals and comprehensions), so lists sometimes contain instances of our Node classes and sometimes the lists themselves are Nodes. (Another issue is that we don't generally support mutating assignment into data structures, though it will work in the first case where you have things like lists of Nodes.)

I think the error here arises from differentiating with respect to a list that itself contains Nodes, but the outer `grad` call doesn't notice the Nodes inside the list (we don't descend recursively into data structures looking for Nodes), leading to getting a Node inside a Node and hence (eventually) an error.

We can fix the problem by making sure the relevant lists are Nodes, rather than native lists that contain Nodes. On the current master branch (not necessarily the pip version that I think you're using), you can use the function `make_list` to do that, which is in `autograd.container_types`. Basically, to be safe, you can call `make_list` wherever you might use Python list syntax, so `[a, b, c]` would become `make_list(a, b, c)`. (Most use cases Just Work™, so we don't advertise these functions very well, but higher order differentiation involving lists like this is one case where they may be necessary.)

I think this version of the code, which calls `make_list` and also avoids mutating assignment into lists, works on the current master branch:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad 
from autograd.container_types import make_list

def objective(y, x):
     return np.sum(np.dot(x[0], y) + x[1])

def sgd(f, y, x):
    g = grad(f, argnum=1)
    for i in range(5):
        x = make_list(*[x[j] - 0.1 * g(y, x)[j] for j in range(2)])
    return f(y, x)

def wrapper(y):
    x = [np.random.randn(1, 3), np.random.randn(1)]
    return np.sum(sgd(objective, y, x))

y = np.random.randn(3, 1)
print("Forward works:  " + str(wrapper(y)))
g = grad(wrapper)
print("Backward works: " + str(g(y)))
```

You might have to use the git master version for that code to work. We'll try to get the pip version updated soon.

I believe your function `f` is not actually scalar-valued as written; it's vector-valued (with the same shape as the input, since `np.exp` acts element-wise). The `hessian` function actually does the standard thing here, which is that it adds two trailing axes (or 'indices') to the type of the output of the function. In this case, since the output of the function has one axis (i.e. is a one-index object), the Hessian has two more axes to make three axes (i.e. it's a three-index object).

In my previous note, I described some terminology as well as details of the existing implementation.

### Back to the original question

Now I will try to answer your question (or at least clarify the terms)

> Should the first set of command line arguments override
>    Command line arguments passed to cmake explicitly? Should the default do so?
>    Command line arguments passed to make explicity? Should the default do so?
>    Arguments passed to setup via kwargs? Should the default do so?

Let's clarify further so that we talk about the same arguments:

* cmake command line arguments corresponds to `cmake_args`: They are passed to cmake for configuring the project  (e.g `-DFOO:STRING=bar`)

* make command line arguments corresponds to `build_tool_args`:
  * They are passed to the build tool like  `make`, `ninja`, or `msbuild`, an example of such parameter is `-jN` for `make` or `/m` for `msbuild`. 

* argument passed via `kwargs`. i am assuming we are talking about `cmake_args` here.


### An example

```
./setup.py bdist_wheel --config RelWithDebInfo -- -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE:STRING=Release
```

The fundamental issue that I have with `@preflight` is that the timing of it is relative to something conceptually outside of the reactive framework but it is expected to block processing of handlers while it runs.  The concept of "mutually exclusive flags," or even "pre-set callbacks" on flags fits better with the framework, in that sense, to me.  However, both end up interrupting the conceptual flow of "when a flag is changed, the handlers that care about it are queued to run," so they both still make me a bit uneasy.  But, obviously there's a definite use-case for it, so my preference would be for the descriptive approach ("these flags are mutually exclusive").  The flag classes should give a good place to implement the behavior without it being at the module level.

> Should handlers be reinvoked if the data attached to their flags changes?

I definitely think we'd need to be able to mark flags as "changed" based on data or some other condition, even if they're already set.

My bad, that's not how `loadscope` works; the idea of using fixtures is one we had, but it is different than what `loadscope` provides.

`loadscope` will automatically group all *test functions* and *test methods* to the same workers, automatically. In your example, all test methods of each class will be sent to separate workers. If you had a single class, then all the methods will be sent to the same worker:

```python
class TestCaseA:
    def test_one(self, fix):
        assert True

    def test_two(self, fix):
        assert True
```

I caught it while customising importer for remote updating when have used original as base class. And was a bit confused :)

The point of freezing the basket is that during payment no other items can be added to it. Otherwise you could initiate payment, add some more items to your basket, finish the payment and have an order that costs more than you paid.

What you should do instead is create a new basket if the current basket is frozen and put all the products from the frozen basket in the new basket as well. This gets problematic if you track stock - then you need a notion of reserving items for some time for checkout. Oscar doesn't implement that fully

@refack - soup is a good description! We have not been very successful in helping folks track along as the Shared Framework and SDK progressed at different rates. Certainly something we are working to remedy in 2.0. 

The primary version split is between the project.json based tools, which we are moving away from, and csproj. The Shared Framework doesn't have any restriction so you can target any version using either project type. The following have been pre-releases of SDK 1.0.

* SDK Preview 2 and 2.1 support project.json
* SDK Preview 3+ and the recent RC3 and RC4 support csproj

1.0.x and 1.1 refer to Share Framework (Microsoft.NETCore.App) version as the .NET Core "umbrella".

I fear that despite the inelegance of adding four more files in the `live/` directory, perhaps we should do that (and also make sure we add a README file at the same time, saying which ones are appropriate for which software).

Part of the reason is that rather than stub installers that spit out certs and chains in funny formats, what we really want is installer plugins for  `haproxy`, `courier-imapd-ssl`, `pure-ftpd` etc.  And we'll be happier if in general those installers don't need to be re-run with full force at renewal time; there's a lower probability of things going wrong if (say) the `haproxy` daemon can just be reloaded to pick up a new cert file from `/etc/letsencrypt/live/` directory.  But that's only possible if the client handed the `haproxy` plugin a set of files that are sufficient for filling all the necessary lines in the haproxy conf file.

On the other hand, it will suck to have private keys in any of the files in `/etc/letsencrypt/live`.  So maybe this can only work if the formats that have keys in them can go in `/etc/letsencrypt/keys` :(

@lawre You are able to parse `CST` likely because your local machine's time zone is Central, and the local time zone abbreviations are in the parser's search path. Generally, 3-letter time zones other than UTC and GMT are not supported because they are by nature ambiguous (CST, for example is overloaded - Cuba Standard Time, Central Standard Time, China Standard Time, etc).

If you want to parse IST correctly, you should construct a `tzinfo` object and pass it to the `tzinfos` parameter of the parser:

```
>>> from dateutil import tz
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>> IST = gettz('Asia/Kolkata')
>>> tzinfos = {'IST': IST}
>>> parse("Sun May  8 03:10:20 IST 2016", tzinfos=tzinfos)
datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 8, 3, 10, 20, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Asia/Kolkata'))
```

When #227 is merged (which will be before the next release), you will be able to avoid using `tzinfos` for things that show are in the Olson database (which are unambiguous time zones), but that will not (and likely will never) include three-letter abbreviations - you'll have to disambiguate them yourself given your specific context.

If you're using `v0.1.6` or before, you have to create a DataFrame that binds explicitely `BG10` to `HIMEDCY`. Something like this sould work :

    # Open and load the data
    d = json.load(open('/home/journois/Téléchargements/sqlexport9658.txt'))

    # Create a DataFrame binding BG10->HIMEDCY for each feature
    data = pd.DataFrame([[feature['properties']['BG10'],feature['properties']['HIMEDCY']]
                 for feature in d['features']], columns=['BG10','HIMEDCY'])

    # Create the map
    m = folium.Map([44.91,-93.12], zoom_start=12)

    # Create the choropleth
    m.geo_json(
        geo_str=json.dumps(d),
        key_on='feature.properties.BG10',
        data=data,
        columns=['BG10','HIMEDCY'],
        fill_color='PuBu',
        threshold_scale=[10000, 50000, 100000, 150000],
        )

If you're on the master (coming `v0.2.0`), you can bind the color on the fly:

    # You have to create your own colormap function.
    # This has to be simplified in the future.
    def PuBu_colormap(x):
        """Colormap binding HIMEDCY to a color of the form "#RRGGBB"."""
        PuBu_colors = folium.utilities.color_brewer('PuBu')
        index = len([y for y in [10000, 50000, 100000, 150000] if y<x])
        return PuBu_colors[index]h

    # Create the map
    m = folium.Map([44.91,-93.12], zoom_start=12)

    # Create the choropleth
    folium.GeoJson(
        d,
        style_function=lambda x: {
            'fillColor' : PuBu_colormap(x['properties']['HIMEDCY']),
            'fillOpacity' : 0.9,
            'color' : 'black',
            'weight' : 2
            },
        ).add_to(m)

The advantage of this new approach is that you can control explicitely each style component.

Simple rule to use grafanalib from bazel could look such way:

```
def _grafanalib_impl(ctx):
  args = []
  args.append("-o")
  args.append(ctx.outputs.json.path)
  args.append(ctx.file.src.path)

  ctx.action(
    executable = ctx.executable.generator,
    inputs = [ctx.file.src],
    arguments = args,
    outputs = [ctx.outputs.json],
  )

grafanalib_build = rule(
  implementation = _grafanalib_impl,
  attrs = {
    'src': attr.label(
      allow_files=True,
      single_file=True
    ),
    'generator': attr.label(
        executable = True,
        cfg = 'host',
        default = '@//buildutils/grafanalib:generator',
    ),
  },
  outputs = {
    "json": "%{name}.json",
  },
)
```

with a wrapper:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

from grafanalib._gen import generate_dashboard_script

if __name__ == '__main__':
    generate_dashboard_script()
```

So it could work the following way:
```
load('//buildutils/grafanalib:grafanalib.bzl', 'grafanalib_build')

grafanalib_build(
  name = "test-board",
  src = "test-board.dashboard.py",
)
```

The schema factory workaround does not provide a `Schema` with the ability to do both `partial` and `not partial` depending on what parameters are provided to `load`.

In other words, when the factory creates a `PartialSchema`, calling `schema.load(partial=False)` will only affect first level fields.

See this example. I added a `partial` parameter to the factory.

```py
from copy import deepcopy

from marshmallow import Schema, fields

def PartialSchemaFactory(schema_cls, partial=True):
    schema = schema_cls(partial=partial)
    for field_name, field in schema.fields.items():
        if isinstance(field, fields.Nested):
            new_field = deepcopy(field)
            new_field.schema.partial = partial
            schema.fields[field_name] = new_field
    return schema

class Address(Schema):
    street = fields.Str(required=True)
    city = fields.Str(required=True)
    state = fields.Str(required=True)
    zipcode = fields.Str(required=True)
    country = fields.Str(required=True)

class User(Schema):
    first_name = fields.Str(required=True)
    last_name = fields.Str(required=True)
    address = fields.Nested(Address)

payload = {
    'first_name': 'John',
    'last_name': 'Doe',
    'address': {
        'zipcode': '90210'
    }
}

user_schema = PartialSchemaFactory(User)
data, errors = user_schema.load(payload, partial=False)
assert errors == {}
```

This passes because `first_name` and `last_name` are provided and `partial=False` is not propagated down.

Maybe a more complete workaround would create two schemas: `PartialSchemaFactory(User, True)` and `PartialSchemaFactory(User, False)`, and pick the right one when calling `load` depending on the value of `partial`. This sounds pretty hackish.

It would be really nice if the `partial` option to `load` was propagated down to nested fields.

Can this be considered a feature request? What do you think of the idea?

Rather than performance, could this be required to keep recursively nested schema from cycling? It was originally undocumented, but later received [documentation](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/commit/90d11fc4813f6604872c0e53933cf3f07c6e39f4).

Something to keep in mind is that the `Nested` field's underlying `__schema` object [is always a schema instance](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L394) not a class, so you shouldn't have to worry about `dump()` mutating any classes, just schema instances that may have been reused to construct `Nested` fields.

I thought that I would point out that while I know very little about Windows, I do know that somehow mark and space parity are supported. I have a number of Windows applications that use it. I could be wrong here, but I do not think that it is emulated by hacking odd and even parity, I think that the OS actually handles it. In systems that hack the parity, only the given bytes that match the parity to the required mark or space are loaded into the transmit buffer. When the hardware signals an interrupt because the transmit buffer is empty, the parity is then flipped and all of the bytes that match the new parity are loaded. The result is that if you watch this on a scope, you see a pause each time that parity has to be changed to maintain maro or space output. If you watch the applications that I am using, there is no such pause, so that suggests that the hardware is getting set into mark or space parity somehow. (Also, I have a bunch of USB->serial converters, some support stick parity and some do not, the ones that do not support stick parity do not work with these applications in Windows. They silently fail and use either odd or even parity instead of stick parity. Refer to the chip docs for the 16550 bit 5 of the LCR. Some chips mark this bit as "reserved".)

The usage of these commands is a little clunky. :wink: I originally implemented it for the Perl version (back when it was the *only* version) for a web based chatbot editor tool, so its usage was geared around that use case:

1. Use `deparse()` to get a Python data structure that you can edit.
2. Use `write(filename, deparsed)` to write your data structure as a RiveScript source file.
3. Use `load_file()` in the future to load that generated source file.

Full example:

```python
deparsed = bot.deparse()
deparsed["topic"]["random"]["hello bot"] = {
    "reply": ["Hello human."],
}
bot.write("custom.rive", deparsed)

# ...
bot.load_file("custom.rive")
```

To make the functions a little more useful, the module can be updated to allow "reversing" the `deparse()` function; for example, if you *give* `deparse()` an equivalent data structure it can put it into the bot's brain.

```python
def deparse(self, deparsed=None):
    if type(deparsed) is dict:
        # loop through it and put things into the bot's memory...
    else:
        # the current logic of exporting the bot's memory and returning it
```

I appreciate this is merged already but I still take issue with it. Before opening a new request, here's my concern:

This change unfortunately breaks existing code and thus is backwards incompatible (previously all GET requests would pass DjangoAuthorization). To fix one's code, the only two options are to either give all users 'change' permission (bad), or to subclass DjangoAuthorization for a custom implementation of the read actions (potentially lots of work). Was this intended?

I appreciate the reasoning behind this in the django admin context, but I doubt setting 'read' as the equivalent to 'change' in an API context is a sensible default assumption as it is not the expected behavior. After all, if GET is an allowed method along with DjangoAuthorization, why would it refuse access based on the fact that the user does not have the `change` permission? 

I propose he following alternative implementation:

_for read_detail_ 

* if the model has a `view` permission, check that (= improvement for those who need it)
* if there is no `view` permission, always allow (= backwards compatible)

_for read_list_

* if the model has a `list` permission, check that (= improvement for those who need it)
* if there is no `list` permission, always allow (= backwards compatible)

This way tastypie adds a default that's compatible with Django's permission defaults while giving an easy path to improve by adding view and list permissions for those who need it, short of writing a custom implementation of DjangoAuthorization.

At least there should be a way to specify the default permission:

```
DjangoAuthorization(read_permission='view', # applies to both list, detail if not spec'd
                    read_list_permission='view', # list only
                    read_detail_permission='view') # detail only
# while we're at it, why not add the other permissions too
DjangoAuthorization(change_permission='change',
                    delete_permission='delete',
                    add_permission='add', 
                    # ... add options as per above for each
                    # <action>_<level> permission where 
                    # action is `change,delete,add,read`,  level is `list,detail`)
```
